name: Taiwan Semiconductor Strait Crisis
description: A military blockade of Taiwan disrupts wafer fabs and maritime lanes carrying advanced semiconductors. Taiwan produces the majority of the world’s contract foundry output; a sustained disruption propagates through electronics, autos, and labor markets.
trigger_event: People’s Liberation Army naval blockade closes Taiwan Strait chip and materials traffic

strait_blockade_chips.event: Taiwan Strait closed to commercial chip and fab inbound traffic
strait_blockade_chips.detail: Industry analysts attribute roughly 60% of global foundry revenue and over 90% of leading-edge (<7 nm) logic to Taiwan (TSMC, UMC). A blockade idles fabs and stops lithography chemistry and wafer deliveries within days.
strait_blockade_chips.unit: % of global foundry capacity at risk
strait_blockade_chips.source: SIA Factbook 2024; TrendForce foundry share data

war_risk_shipping.event: Maritime war-risk premiums and rerouting add weeks of lead time
war_risk_shipping.detail: Baltic Exchange war-risk pricing for the Strait spiked in prior Red Sea–style shocks by triple-digit percent; carriers divert via longer routes or idle.
war_risk_shipping.source: Reuters shipping insurance reporting; IMF trade disruption notes

wafer_inbound_stop.event: Photoresist, specialty gases, and wafer stockpiles deplete at fabs
wafer_inbound_stop.detail: Leading-edge fabs typically hold limited buffer; TSMC has noted supply-chain concentration in materials. Disruption beyond 2–4 weeks forces utilization cuts industry-wide.
wafer_inbound_stop.source: Company 10-K supply disclosures; SEMI materials reports

logic_wafer_shortage.event: Advanced-node logic and memory allocation rationed to OEMs
logic_wafer_shortage.detail: Smartphones, PCs, and servers compete for the same leading-edge wafers; historical 2020–2022 chip shortage added ~$210B in lost auto revenue per AlixPartners estimates.

oem_bom_inflation.event: Electronics bill-of-materials costs jump for phones, PCs, servers
oem_bom_inflation.detail: During 2021–2022, discrete component price spikes passed through; server and GPU pricing rose sharply amid allocation.
oem_bom_inflation.source: IDC hardware pricing commentary; Gartner IT spending data

retail_device_price_spike.detail: Historical correlation: when memory and logic tighten, PC and handset ASPs increase; OEMs cite component costs in earnings.
retail_device_price_spike.source: Consumer Technology Association trend data; OEM earnings calls

automotive_mcu_crisis.event: Vehicle assembly lines slow for lack of MCUs and power semis
automotive_mcu_crisis.detail: 2021 chip shortage cut global light-vehicle output by ~11 million units (IHS/S&P estimates). Taiwan-heavy supply magnifies a repeat.
automotive_mcu_crisis.unit: million vehicles output at risk (analog magnitude)
automotive_mcu_crisis.source: S&P Global Mobility; McKinsey automotive semiconductor analysis

auto_tier_layoffs.event: Automotive and electronics suppliers announce workforce reductions
auto_tier_layoffs.detail: During 2022–2023 tech retrenchment, US tech layoffs exceeded 150k in peak-year trackers; auto supplier cuts tracked production schedules.

services_inflation_feedthrough.event: Sticky services and repair inflation as durable goods stay expensive
services_inflation_feedthrough.detail: Core CPI persistence in 2022–2024 showed services lagging goods; high device and vehicle costs extend replacement cycles and repair demand.
services_inflation_feedthrough.source: Bureau of Labor Statistics CPI; Fed staff research on persistence

personal_device_budget.event: Your spending on phones, laptops, and upgrades rises
personal_device_budget.detail: Direct pass-through from higher ASPs and fewer promotions; households delay refresh cycles or pay more at purchase.

personal_real_income_squeeze.event: Your real wages buy less as layoff risk and inflation combine
personal_real_income_squeeze.detail: Joint effect of higher goods prices and labor-market slack in exposed sectors on household budgets.
